reasoning: The finding correctly identifies that ${keyInfo} on line 6 of encrypted-key.tpl.xml.js is interpolated without escapehtml(), while adjacent fields use escapehtml(). However, this is intentional design: keyInfo is a pre-rendered XML fragment (the full output of the keyinfo template), not a raw string — escaping it would corrupt the XML structure. The actual unescaped interpolation of caller-controlled data occurs one level earlier: ${encryptionPublicCert} in keyinfo.tpl.xml.js line 10, where the PEM cert body (from options.pem) is spliced raw into the KeyInfo XML block without escaping. The finding mislabels both the location (citing encrypted-key.tpl.xml.js line 6 as the sink rather than keyinfo.tpl.xml.js line 10) and the direct trigger variable. More critically, the reachability claim fails adversarial scrutiny: options.pem is a server-side configuration parameter (the encryption certificate), not a value sourced from HTTP request input or any user-controlled channel. No schema validation is present, but the parameter is consumed only by trusted server-side callers who configure the library. There is no realistic path from a remote attacker to options.pem without already having process-level access. The finding conflates library API caller trust with attacker control. No non-default options are needed, but the prerequisite — attacker-controlled options.pem — does not exist in any realistic deployment model. Verdict: the cited sink and location are wrong, and the reachability is not demonstrated.
